skill.name: linux-privilege-escalation
skill.description: Systematic privilege escalation assessment skill for identifying and exploiting Linux misconfigurations, vulnerable services, and security weaknesses to elevate from low-privilege access to root-level control.
skill.summary: Comprehensive Linux privilege escalation toolkit covering kernel exploits, sudo misconfigurations, SUID binaries, cron jobs, capabilities, and NFS weaknesses for authorized penetration testing.
skill.author: zebbern

security_audit.summary: This skill contains complete weaponized exploitation workflows for Linux privilege escalation. While framed as educational content for penetration testing, it provides ready-to-use commands for gaining unauthorized root access, credential theft via /etc/shadow access, SUID backdoor creation, kernel exploitation, and persistent reverse shell payloads. The static scanner correctly identified 44 high-risk patterns including sudo exploitation, SUID manipulation, and shadow file access. False positives include Ruby backtick detection (actually bash commands in markdown). This content should only be distributed in controlled professional security contexts with explicit authorization requirements.

critical_findings.title: Credential File Access for Password Theft
critical_findings.description: Direct access to /etc/shadow and /etc/passwd files for credential harvesting. The skill teaches reading shadow hashes and cracking them with John the Ripper, enabling unauthorized password recovery for any system user.

critical_findings.title: SUID Backdoor Creation
critical_findings.description: Instructions for creating persistent SUID root shells that provide permanent backdoor access. The skill teaches copying /bin/bash and setting the SUID bit, allowing any user to gain root access indefinitely.

critical_findings.title: Kernel Exploitation with Crash Risk
critical_findings.description: Complete kernel exploit workflow including Dirty COW and Dirty Pipe exploitation. The skill acknowledges failed exploits may crash the system (line 433), indicating dangerous unstable code execution.

critical_findings.title: Reverse Shell Payload Library
critical_findings.description: Multiple ready-to-use reverse shell payloads for bash, Python, Netcat, and Perl. These one-liners enable covert remote access and are commonly used in real attacks for persistent unauthorized access.

high_findings.title: Sudo Misconfiguration Exploitation
high_findings.description: Comprehensive guide to exploiting sudo permissions via GTFOBins techniques. Teaches escaping to root shell through common binaries like vim, find, awk, and python when available via sudo.

high_findings.title: LD_PRELOAD Shared Library Injection
high_findings.description: Instructions for creating malicious shared libraries to hijack sudo execution. The provided C code sets uid/gid to 0 and spawns a root shell when loaded via LD_PRELOAD.

high_findings.title: UID/GID Manipulation for Root Access
high_findings.description: Direct manipulation of process credentials using cap_setuid capability. Python, vim, and perl commands shown that set uid(0) to gain root privileges without password.

high_findings.title: Cron Job Hijacking
high_findings.description: Techniques for modifying writable cron scripts to execute attacker commands as root. Includes adding reverse shell payloads to backup scripts that run with root privileges.

high_findings.title: PATH Hijacking Attack
high_findings.description: Instructions for exploiting SUID binaries that call external commands by placing malicious executables in writable PATH directories. Creates fake 'service' command to spawn root shell.

high_findings.title: NFS no_root_squash Exploitation
high_findings.description: Exploits NFS shares configured with no_root_squash to create SUID binaries on mounted shares. When executed on target, the binary runs as root providing unauthorized access.

medium_findings.title: Automated Reconnaissance Script Execution
medium_findings.description: Pipes curl directly to shell for LinPEAS execution. While common in penetration testing, this pattern downloads and executes unverified code which could be intercepted or modified.

medium_findings.title: Network Service Enumeration
medium_findings.description: Extensive network reconnaissance commands including netstat, ss, and ip route enumeration. These could be used to map internal network topology for lateral movement.

medium_findings.title: User Enumeration via passwd File
medium_findings.description: Commands to enumerate users with login shells and home directories. While /etc/passwd is world-readable, this information facilitates targeted attacks.

low_findings.title: System Information Gathering
low_findings.description: Basic enumeration commands for kernel version, hostname, and architecture. Standard reconnaissance that is necessary for vulnerability matching but low risk in isolation.

low_findings.title: Process and Service Enumeration
low_findings.description: Commands to list running processes and identify services running as root. Useful for identifying exploitation targets but benign administrative commands.

low_findings.title: Capability Enumeration
low_findings.description: Uses getcap to enumerate Linux capabilities on binaries. Standard security auditing command that identifies potential escalation vectors.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic Code Execution via os.system
dangerous_patterns.description: Python os.system() calls with hardcoded shell commands for privilege escalation

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Execution via awk
dangerous_patterns.description: AWK BEGIN block executing system commands to spawn shells

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Callback via /dev/tcp
dangerous_patterns.description: Bash reverse shell using /dev/tcp device for outbound connection

dangerous_patterns.title: SUID Permission Modification
dangerous_patterns.description: chmod +s commands to set SUID bit on binaries for privilege escalation

content.value_statement: Security professionals need systematic methods to identify privilege escalation vectors on Linux systems during authorized penetration tests. This skill provides comprehensive enumeration and exploitation workflows covering kernel vulnerabilities, sudo misconfigurations, SUID binaries, and cron job weaknesses.
actual_capabilities: Enumerate system information including kernel version, users, groups, and network configuration
Identify sudo misconfigurations exploitable via GTFOBins techniques
Find and exploit SUID binaries for privilege escalation
Detect kernel vulnerabilities and suggest applicable exploits
Enumerate and exploit cron jobs, capabilities, and NFS misconfigurations
Generate reverse shell payloads for multiple languages and tools
limitations: Requires existing shell access to target Linux system
Kernel exploits may crash systems and should be tested first
Modern mitigations like ASLR, SMEP, SMAP may block exploitation
Container environments limit many kernel-level techniques

use_cases.title: Authorized Penetration Testing
use_cases.description: Security consultants performing internal network assessments use this skill to systematically identify privilege escalation paths and demonstrate business impact of initial access.
use_cases.target_user: Penetration testers and red team operators

use_cases.title: Security Audit and Compliance
use_cases.description: System administrators audit their own infrastructure to identify and remediate misconfigurations before attackers can exploit them.

use_cases.title: Security Training and Education
use_cases.description: Security professionals studying for certifications like OSCP, GPEN, or CRT use this skill to understand common Linux privilege escalation techniques in lab environments.

prompt_templates.title: Basic System Enumeration
prompt_templates.prompt: I have low-privilege shell access to a Linux system. Help me enumerate the system to identify potential privilege escalation vectors including kernel version, sudo permissions, SUID binaries, and running services.
prompt_templates.scenario: Initial reconnaissance after gaining shell access

prompt_templates.title: Sudo Privilege Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: I can run 'sudo -l' and see I have NOPASSWD access to specific binaries. Analyze the output and provide GTFOBins exploitation techniques for each allowed command.

prompt_templates.title: SUID Binary Exploitation
prompt_templates.prompt: Find all SUID binaries on this system and identify which ones can be exploited for privilege escalation. Provide specific exploitation commands for each vulnerable binary.

prompt_templates.title: Kernel Exploit Selection
prompt_templates.prompt: The kernel version is [VERSION]. Search for known exploits applicable to this kernel and provide compilation and execution instructions. Include fallback options if the primary exploit fails.

output_examples.input: User runs sudo -l and finds they can execute /usr/bin/find as root
output_examples.output: GTFOBins exploitation: Run 'sudo find . -exec /bin/bash \; -quit' to spawn a root shell. The find command's -exec flag allows arbitrary command execution with root privileges.

Exploit: Use 'base64 /etc/shadow | base64 -d > shadow.txt' to extract password hashes. Transfer shadow.txt to attacker machine and crack with 'john --wordlist=rockyou.txt shadow.txt' to recover plaintext passwords.

output_examples.input: Root cron job executes writable script at /opt/scripts/backup.sh
output_examples.output: Hijack: Append 'cp /bin/bash /tmp/bash; chmod +s /tmp/bash' to the script. After cron executes, run '/tmp/bash -p' for persistent root access via SUID backdoor.

best_practices: Always obtain written authorization before testing and verify scope boundaries
Test kernel exploits in a lab environment before production use to avoid system crashes
Document all changes made during assessment for remediation and cleanup
anti_patterns: Running kernel exploits without understanding their mechanism or failure modes
Creating persistent backdoors beyond the authorized assessment period
Accessing or exfiltrating data beyond what is required to demonstrate privilege escalation

faq.question: Is this skill legal to use?
faq.answer: Only use this skill on systems you own or have explicit written authorization to test. Unauthorized privilege escalation is illegal in most jurisdictions. Always operate within defined scope boundaries.

faq.question: Will kernel exploits work on all Linux systems?
faq.answer: No. Modern kernels include mitigations like ASLR, SMEP, and SMAP that block many exploits. Container environments also limit kernel-level attacks. Always verify kernel version and test exploits first.

faq.question: What should I do if an exploit crashes the system?
faq.answer: This is why testing in lab environments is critical. If a crash occurs during authorized testing, document the incident, restore from backup if needed, and report it to the system owner immediately.

faq.question: How do I know if a SUID binary is exploitable?
faq.answer: Check GTFOBins (gtfobins.github.io) for the specific binary. Many common binaries like find, vim, python, and base64 have known exploitation techniques when running with SUID.

faq.question: Can this skill be used in cloud environments like AWS or Azure?
faq.answer: Cloud instances have additional restrictions. Kernel exploits typically fail in containers. Focus on misconfigurations like sudo permissions, capabilities, and IAM role abuse instead.

faq.question: What is the difference between this skill and tools like LinPEAS?
faq.answer: LinPEAS automates enumeration but does not provide exploitation guidance. This skill covers both enumeration AND provides specific exploitation commands for identified vulnerabilities.
